For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
title: Deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for exact matching in bipartite graphs
background_latex:
Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph in which every edge is colored either red or blue, and let \(|A|=|B|\). A matching \(M\subseteq E\) is perfect if every vertex in \(A\cup B\) is incident to exactly one edge of \(M\). For a perfect matching \(M\), let \(r(M)\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\).

The \(\textit{exact matching problem}\) asks, given \((G,k)\) with integer \(k\), whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\) with \(r(M)=k\). The paper studies a different class of problems (popular near-maximum-weight matchings) and proves that if one could solve a certain popularity-constrained matching problem deterministically in polynomial time, then one could solve the exact matching problem deterministically in polynomial time. The paper notes that no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is known for exact matching.
problem_definition_latex: Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given a bipartite graph \(G=(A,B;E)\) with red/blue edge colors and an integer \(k\), decides whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\subseteq E\) such that \(r(M)=k\) (i.e., \(M\) contains exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) when it exists.
latex:
Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph in which every edge is colored either red or blue, and let \(|A|=|B|\). A matching \(M\subseteq E\) is perfect if every vertex in \(A\cup B\) is incident to exactly one edge of \(M\). For a perfect matching \(M\), let \(r(M)\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\).

The \(\textit{exact matching problem}\) asks, given \((G,k)\) with integer \(k\), whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\) with \(r(M)=k\). The paper studies a different class of problems (popular near-maximum-weight matchings) and proves that if one could solve a certain popularity-constrained matching problem deterministically in polynomial time, then one could solve the exact matching problem deterministically in polynomial time. The paper notes that no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is known for exact matching.

Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given a bipartite graph \(G=(A,B;E)\) with red/blue edge colors and an integer \(k\), decides whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\subseteq E\) such that \(r(M)=k\) (i.e., \(M\) contains exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) when it exists.
location_in_paper: Page 6 (Introduction, discussion before Theorem 4) and page 22 (Section 5.1, statement of Theorem 4 and surrounding text).
open_problem_quote: For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an algorithm-existence question over all bipartite graphs rather than a falsifiable universal inequality; there is no finite counterexample to search for. Instance generation can test heuristics but cannot refute existence of an algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could search for practical deterministic algorithms/heuristics and evaluate them on many random and adversarial instances with a verifiable oracle (e.g., via randomized isolation or exponential verification for small sizes). However, demonstrating a true polynomial-time algorithm with correctness for all instances requires proofs beyond empirical fitness.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
